div.head: CO–36. Memorandum of a ConversationSource: Department of State, Central Files. Secret; Official-Informal. 2 pages not declassified.

frus:attachment.head: Letter from the Ambassador in Colombia (McIntosh) to the Special Assistant in the Bureau of Inter-American Affairs (Devine)The source text is a carbon copy bearing Ambassador McIntosh’s typed name.
frus:attachment.p: I refer to your letter of August 31Not found in Department of State files. enclosing copies of draft papers [text not declassified] proposing ways and means of responding to President Lleras’ request for assistance in combatting the violence and banditry problem. Your letter and these papers have been most helpful to me and to other members of the Country Team in understanding the problem confronted by the Department [text not declassified].

I heartily agree that the ideal solution would be to come up with an agreed upon and mutually accepted program on the basis of which Presidential determination for use of the necessary funds could be sought. However, [text not declassified] it appears that the Department must take the responsibility of proposing an assistance program which, on the one hand, will be responsive to President Lleras’ request and, on the other hand, will form the basis for requesting Presidential determination on the use of MAP.

Urgency is of the essence. The Colombian Government officially requested our decision some 15 months ago. Our only answer to date has been to send a Survey Team to Colombia for the dual purpose of ascertaining whether the violence situation justified our seeking a Presidential determination for the use of Mutual Security Act assistance and to come up with possible recommendations for eventual use by the Colombian Government in the anti-guerrilla campaign. While the GOC cooperated fully and whole-heartedly with the Team, and the
team concluded its field survey last December, we as yet have not submitted to President Lleras for the GOC’s possible guidance and recommendation pursuant to
the Survey team study. Therefore, I believe it is urgent that we now take a decision one way or another and so inform President Lleras. Whether the recommended program will produce the desired results within his term of office is of secondary consideration. His government, in any case, would receive political credit for any successful program launched under his administration. Also, I consider it urgent that we fulfill our commitment to give President Lleras a report of the Survey Team findings—that is, general observations and recommendations for the GOC’s consideration – apart from the question of assistance from us in the form of training and material.

I think it is desirable at this point to review the history and nature of Lleras’ request for assistance. For this purpose I refer you to the Embassy’s Despatches 864 of June 24, 1959Despatch 864, not printed, conveyed background information relating to President Lleras Camargo’s interest in securing MAP assistance from the United States for the purpose of maintaining internal security against guerilla warfare in Colombia. (721.00/6–2459) and 1 of July 1, 1959,See footnote 5 to telegram 439 from Bogotá, March 25, 1960, Document CO–26. the letter which transmitted the GOC’s request for certain material. Earlier President Lleras had urged that we provide helicopters. His request was essentially of two parts: (1) a request for specific material and equipment; and (2) a request that we review our policy of military assistance to Latin America (not making Colombia an exemption) with the idea of removing restrictions on the use of MAP for internal security purposes. President Lleras expressed to us on several occasions, both here and during his official visit to Washington, his belief that our present policy of military assistance for purposes of hemisphere defense only is outmoded—that it would be a better policy to reorientate training of Latin American military establishments toward internal security and counter-guerrilla warfare to cope with movements such as that which took place in Cuba—the sort of thing most likely to threaten the stability of Latin American governments in the future. It might be added that idea is growing in Colombian military circles (see Embassy’s despatch 624 of May 2, 1960).Despatch 624, not printed, transmitted a memorandum reporting a conservation between Lt. Col. Howard C. Parker, Army Attaché to the Embassy in Colombia, and Brig. Gen. Alberto Rueda Teran, Secretary to the Colombian Minister of War, concerning the Colombian Armed Forces primary mission in the area of internal security. (721.5/5–260)

With the foregoing history in mind, the Country Team and I [text not declassified] are of the [text not declassified] opinion that, as between the two proposed programs, the Defense Department paper should be adopted as the basis for a program of assistance to Colombia to combat the violence situation. [text not declassified]

This Country Team recommendation is based on our considered view that the violence problem is essentially a national problem which has
developed to the extent that a military action by the armed forces is required. Thus, this is a military rather than a police problem; and suppression of violence is primarily a military task in which police action should be the secondary role of maintaining order in pacified areas and of participating in direct anti-guerrilla action only where bandit activity is sporadic. Another consideration is that the Defense Department proposal is more responsive to President Lleras’ request, both as regards equipment and as regards reorientation of our military assistance program in general. In none of the numerous conferences between President Lleras and U.S. officials was it ever suggested that he had in mind the use of the National Police in connection with the requested assistance. On the contrary, he initiated the formal request by calling in the chiefs of our three military missions, at which meeting Colombian military personnel were present but not representatives of the National Police (Despatch 864 of June 24, 1959). During this and many subsequent meetings on the subject, President Lleras has spoken only of military assistance and the need for removing the restrictions on the use of MAP for internal security. Not once has the question of utilizing the National Police as the primary medium for anti-violence operations been raised.

The Embassy always has thought that any assistance should be handled through our established military missions and that a separate anti-guerrilla mission would be questionable from a political as well as a practical standpoint. A police mission would identify us directly with the issue of internal order, whereas our efforts to the same end would be less obvious is undertaken through our present military assistance programs. Our MAP is being reoriented along the lines suggested by President Lleras with more emphasis being placed on anti-guerrilla training and material. The armed forces, while being used to solve the violence problem, are, at the same time, being better prepared for their hemisphere defense mission by reason of their training and use in direct anti-guerrilla action, which contributs to hemispheric defense.

The Country Team also sees other possible objections to the use of the National Police as the chosen medium. The political wisdom of assigning U.S. personnel to the National Police on a specific project of violence suppression is very questionable, regardless of whether the National Police remains under military control or is transferred to civilian control. Another complicating, although not overriding, factor is the presence of a Chilean police mission which has been employed as an advisory group for a number of years. Also, the prestige of the National Police is relatively low; conversely, the military reputation in the eyes of the public is better and the military establishment by and
large is recognized as non-political. This position would
not preclude the assignment of individual U.S. advisors [text not declassified] nor the provision of training, material and equipment to the National Police as steps to strengthen the police capability to support the over-all program.

The Country Team concurs in the observations contained in both papers that improve intelligence would be of great help in the solution of the problem. This applies equally to the National Police, DAS, and the military services. I believe that as a part of our program we should offer the services of any advisers the GOC may desire to strengthen the intelligence activity and coordination of these agencies. In fact, the GOC already has requested, through NAAC channels, that our Army mission be augmented by two intelligence officers.

The objectives stated in both papers are very similar. The objectives as stated [text not declassified] (a) “immediate suppression and liquidation of bandits,” and (c) “long-range maintenance of internal security” are similar and are directly responsive to President Lleras’ request. Those objectives address themselves to the problem raised by the GOC appeal for assistance. [text not declassified] I question whether we, with U.S. personnel, should become involved in a special project of rehabilitation as part of an anti-violence program. Rather, I think our efforts should be channeled through existing and contemplated ICA, DLF, and EXIM Bank, social development fund, and international financial agency programs in the fields of colonization, land reform, housing, penetration roads, and other types of financial and technical assistance to Colombia in its attempts to deal with its economic and social program. We are now actually engaged in such an aid program, witness our ICA technical assistance, the $70 million in credits recently approved by the EXIM Bank and the DLF and the continuing flow of credits from the IBRD and DLF. And we have every reason to think, in the light of the position taken by the U.S. Delegation at the Committee of 21 Meeting here, that this type if aid will continue and increase in the future. [text not declassified]

[text not declassified] However, from all information available to the Embassy, including the opinions of our Military Mission and Armed Service personnel, light aircraft are of doubtful usefulness in anti-bandit operations and certainly are not considered to be adequate by the Colombian military. In fact, the only support I have encountered for the use of light aircraft came from certain members of the Survey Team. Admittedly, the cost factor is of import. However, would it not be a waste of money to provide aircraft that would not do the job? The question of exactly what military material and equipment are needed is also a technical one. It is significant, I think, that the equipment listed in the Survey Team report and the material originally requested by the GOC (Despatch 1, July 1, 1959) are very similar.

All who have studied the problem agree that what is needed most is transportation, communication and possibly other specialized material. A few small obsolete helicopters now in the Colombian Air Force have been used successfully in a number of anti-bandit operations, and no doubt if they were available in sufficient numbers and with sufficient lead and altitude capability the Colombian military would have the mobility badly needed to meet violence when and where it occurs, regardless of terrain.

Both papers recognize the need of a public relations program to help civilian understanding and support in violence areas. Such a program would supplement the work of the Equipoz Polivalentes. We agree in principle on the need for a coordinated public relations effort in the anti-violence campaign. However, President Lleras has indicated a personal aversion to government propaganda (perhaps because government propaganda has so frequently been discredited in the past) and I have reasonable doubts that he would accept the proposal for a public relations adviser per se—especially if the latter were to be a foreign national. Also, I think it would be politically objectionable for the U.S. to be directly and overtly involved in this activity. I think any advise we give on public relations as related to the anti-violence program, should be confirmed to such recommendations as can be given in a report to the President and to such ad hoc guidance as can be given through our established U.S. missions to Colombia.

I agree with your personal reaction that neither of the two papers seems to represent a very comprehensive presentation. For your information the military Advisory Group to Colombia at the direction of the Commander in Chief Caribbean (stemming from a DOD request) is preparing recommendations for equipment and training under the MAP program for
FY 1961 to combat this banditry problem. These recommendations are being coordinated with a Colombian Armed Forces plan to combat banditry. In making these recommendations every effort is being made to insure that the whole Colombian Government, especially the Army and Air Force, will coordinate in using the equipment against banditry in an organized and coordinated manner. These recommendations will be cleared with me before going forward through military channels. It is expected that these recommendations will form the basis for a specific DOD proposal on how to help Colombia and that they will be appropriately coordinated with the State Department in Washington.

I apologize for the length of the letter. Nevertheless, the history and background of the problem is such that I think the above comments at this may be helpful to you and to those in the Department directly concerned.

In recapitulation, I recommend that the Defense Department paper be taken as a basis for a proposed program which may be submitted promptly to President Eisenhower for his determination on the use of necessary funds; [text not declassified] and that recommendations for public relations, intelligence, and civil action supplementing the program be included in a report of the Survey Team for delivery to President Lleras, independent of what action we take on the GOC request for assistance in the form of material and training.
